title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about wildlife conservation. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Return
content.subtitle: What happened when wolves came back to the Carpathian forest
content.text:
For decades, the Carpathian forest had been a quieter place than nature intended. The absence of wolves — hunted to local extinction in the region by the mid-twentieth century — had altered the landscape in ways that took ecologists years to fully understand. Deer populations had grown unchecked, grazing riverbanks bare, destabilising the soil, and reducing the forest floor to a thin, impoverished layer of vegetation. [31]It was only when scientists began studying the relationship between predator absence and ecosystem change that the true cost of the wolf's disappearance became clear: it was not simply a missing species but a missing force.[/31]

The decision to reintroduce wolves to the region in 2009 was controversial from the start. [32]Local farmers, whose livelihoods depended on livestock, argued that the programme placed an unreasonable burden on rural communities who had not been adequately consulted and who would bear the practical consequences of the policy.[/32] Conservation organisations countered that compensation schemes and protective measures would address the economic concerns, and that the long-term ecological benefits far outweighed the short-term disruption.

In the years following reintroduction, ecologists observed a phenomenon that has since become widely cited in conservation literature. [33]As wolf numbers increased, deer began to avoid the open riverbanks where they had previously grazed freely, retreating to higher, wooded terrain. Within a decade, the riverbanks had regenerated — grasses, shrubs, and young trees returning to stabilise the soil and improve water quality in the streams below.[/33] The wolves had, in effect, redesigned the landscape simply by being present.

This process, known as a trophic cascade, illustrates how the removal or restoration of a single species can restructure an entire ecosystem. [34]What surprised researchers most was not that the cascade occurred — this had been observed in similar projects elsewhere — but the speed at which it happened. Recovery, it seemed, was faster than anyone had predicted, suggesting that ecosystems have a greater capacity for self-repair than previously assumed.[/34]

The Carpathian project has since become a model for conservation efforts in other parts of Europe. [35]However, ecologists are careful to warn against assuming that reintroduction alone is sufficient. Success requires long-term monitoring, ongoing management of human-wildlife conflict, and — critically — the sustained support of local communities who live alongside the animals.[/35]

[36]The story of the Carpathian wolves is ultimately not about wolves at all, but about interconnection. It demonstrates that in a functioning ecosystem, every component affects every other, and that simplifying nature — even with the best of intentions — carries consequences that may take generations to fully appreciate.[/36]

questions.question: What had scientists discovered about the disappearance of wolves from the Carpathian forest?
options: A) That the forest had benefited economically from the resulting increase in deer numbers.
B) That the absence of wolves had unexpectedly improved plant diversity in some areas.
C) That losing the wolves had removed a fundamental organising influence on the ecosystem.
D) That local farmers had been primarily responsible for the decline in vegetation quality.
questions.explanation: Scientists found that the wolf's disappearance was 'not simply a missing species but a missing force,' meaning its absence destabilised the whole ecosystem.

questions.question: Why did local farmers oppose the wolf reintroduction programme?
options: A) They believed wolves posed a direct physical danger to people living in the area.
B) They felt the decision had been made without properly involving those most affected.
C) They argued that the government compensation offered was completely insufficient.
D) They claimed that wolves had never historically lived in the region at all.
questions.explanation: Farmers argued the programme placed an 'unreasonable burden on rural communities who had not been adequately consulted,' highlighting lack of proper involvement.

questions.question: What evidence showed that the return of wolves had changed the landscape?
options: A) Deer moved away from riverbanks, allowing vegetation to recover and improve water quality.
B) Wolf numbers increased so rapidly that deer populations declined to dangerously low levels.
C) Farmers reported seeing fewer deer on their land than at any point in the previous century.
D) Scientists measured a significant decrease in the total area of forest within the region.
questions.explanation: The text describes deer retreating from riverbanks, which then regenerated with grasses, shrubs and trees, stabilising the soil and improving stream water quality.

questions.question: What most surprised researchers about the trophic cascade in the Carpathian forest?
options: A) The fact that a trophic cascade had never previously been recorded in any project.
B) The extent to which wolves interfered directly with agricultural activities in the area.
C) The unexpected negative effects the cascade had on certain plant species.
D) The pace at which the ecosystem began to repair itself after the wolves returned.
questions.explanation: Researchers were surprised 'not that the cascade occurred' but by 'the speed at which it happened,' suggesting ecosystems self-repair faster than assumed.

questions.question: What does the article suggest is necessary for wildlife reintroduction to succeed?
options: A) Preventing all human activity in areas where reintroduced animals are living.
B) Ensuring that reintroduced animals are monitored by scientists at all times.
C) Combining reintroduction with community engagement and continued active management.
D) Releasing as many animals as possible to establish a stable population quickly.
questions.explanation: The text says success requires 'long-term monitoring, ongoing management of human-wildlife conflict, and the sustained support of local communities.'

questions.question: What broader point does the final paragraph make about ecosystems?
options: A) That the recovery of ecosystems is inevitable once human interference ceases completely.
B) That all parts of a natural system are linked, and changes in one affect all others.
C) That wolves are uniquely important compared to other species in forest environments.
D) That nature conservation should be the primary concern of all European governments.
questions.explanation: The final paragraph states that 'in a functioning ecosystem, every component affects every other,' making the story one of interconnection rather than just wolves.
